app.title: Neural Network from Scratch
app.subtitle: An interactive visualization of a 2-4-1 feedforward network trained on XOR. Green connections are positive weights, red are negative.
app.description1: A <1>neural network</1> is a tiny computer brain made of virtual neurons. Each neuron receives numbers, multiplies them by little knobs called <2>weights</2>, adds a bias, and passes the result through an activation function that decides whether the neuron should "fire" strongly or stay quiet.
app.description2: This demo shows a network with <1>2 inputs</1>, <2>4 hidden neurons</2>, and <3>1 output</3>. It is learning the XOR pattern — a classic puzzle that a simple line cannot solve. You can drag the sliders to give the brain any two numbers, press <4>Train on XOR</4> to let it practice, and watch the weights change colour and thickness as they find the right answer.
app.description3: Everything you see is computed right here in your browser using the same algorithm as the Zig implementation in the parent repository. No cloud, no library — just loops, matrices, and a little patience.

controls.howItWorksLine1: Imagine a tiny robot brain with 2 ears and 1 mouth.
controls.howItWorksLine2: The <1>two sliders</1> are like dimmer switches. Slide them to 0 (off) or 1 (on) or anything in between.
controls.howItWorksLine3: The <1>brain</1> sees the two numbers and makes a guess. The guess appears in the blue box below.
controls.howItWorksLine4: A guess near <1>0</1> means the brain thinks "off." A guess near <1>1</1> means it thinks "on."
controls.howItWorksLine5: When you press <1>Train on XOR</1>, the brain practices with flashcards until it learns the secret pattern!

matrixDemo.title: How Matrix Multiplication Works
matrixDemo.subtitle: A neural network multiplies matrices just like this. Watch each row meet each column to make one answer cell.
matrixDemo.allDone: All done! Every answer cell is filled.

matrixDefinition.title: Matrix Definition
matrixDefinition.description: A neural network is built on matrices. We store each matrix as a flat Float32Array in row-major order: element (r, c) is at index r * cols + c. This matches the Zig implementation exactly.

matrixMultiplication.title: Matrix Multiplication
matrixMultiplication.description: The heart of a neural network is the dot product: z = W · x. This triple-nested loop multiplies an (m×n) matrix by an (n×p) matrix, producing an (m×p) result. No BLAS, no SIMD—just the raw algorithm so you can trace every multiplication.

activationFunctions.title: Activation Functions
activationFunctions.description: Activation functions introduce non-linearity. Without them, stacking layers would be equivalent to a single linear transform. We use Leaky ReLU for hidden layers (prevents "dead neurons") and sigmoid for the output layer (gives a probability between 0 and 1).

heInitialisation.title: He Initialisation
heInitialisation.description: Weight initialisation matters. If weights are too large, neurons saturate and gradients vanish. If too small, signals die out. He initialisation scales random weights by sqrt(2 / fan_in), preserving variance across layers.

forwardPass.title: Forward Pass
forwardPass.description: Forward propagation is simple: for each layer, compute z = W·x + b, then apply the activation function a = f(z). We cache both z and a because backpropagation needs them later.

backpropOutput.title: Backpropagation — Output Layer
backpropOutput.description: The chain rule tells us how much each weight contributed to the error. For the output layer: δ = (a - y) ⊙ f'(z). This is the error signal we will send backwards through the network.

backpropHidden.title: Backpropagation — Hidden Layers
backpropHidden.description: For hidden layers, we cannot compare directly to the target. Instead we "propagate the blame" backwards: δ_l = (W_{l+1}ᵀ · δ_{l+1}) ⊙ f'(z_l). The transpose of the next layer's weights sends the error signal back through the connections.

gradientDescent.title: Gradient Descent Update
gradientDescent.description: Finally, we adjust every weight in the direction that reduces loss. The gradient ∂Loss/∂W is the outer product of the error signal δ and the previous layer's activation a. We step downhill: W_new = W_old - lr · gradient.
